title: Users describe a self-reinforcing loop—but counterevidence prevents a universal-failure claim.
objective: Explain the likely behavioral mechanism through cited examples while keeping alternative explanations visible.

layout.template: Behavior loop with three evidence callouts
layout.right: Two complaint examples plus one counterexample
layout.bottom: What is observed versus what remains a hypothesis

observed_patterns: Large libraries can feel small: one user reports roughly 100 recurring tracks from a 4,000-song library.
Generated radio and playlists can return listeners to music they already play rather than introduce useful novelty.
Users protect the profile through private sessions, exclusions, manual playlists, external discovery, or even clean accounts.
Temporary contexts such as children’s music can influence later recommendations and feel difficult to repair.
Some long-tenured users explicitly ask for a familiar-versus-pure-discovery setting.

evidence_callouts.label: Experienced repetition
evidence_callouts.paraphrase: A 4,000-song Liked Songs library feels like the same roughly 100 tracks; the user reports more skipping than listening.

evidence_callouts.label: Requested control
evidence_callouts.paraphrase: A listener proposes choosing between songs already liked and pure discovery of unheard material.

evidence_callouts.label: Counterevidence
evidence_callouts.paraphrase: Other users report good Discover Weekly or Daylist outcomes after deliberately varying inputs and managing profile signals.

content.leading_hypothesis: Persistent listening history cannot fully express changing session intent; poor correction causes familiar fallback, which supplies more familiar history.
alternative_explanations: Shuffle or playback defects rather than discovery ranking
content.research_implication: Interviews must observe a recent failure and separate these mechanisms before the product scope is locked.

visuals.alt: Want something new leads to familiar results, skipping, familiar fallback, stronger familiar history, and the next narrow discovery attempt.

speaker_notes: This is the causal bridge, but label it a hypothesis every time.
Counterevidence is strategically useful: we should augment a working taste anchor, not replace personalization.
Use paraphrases on-slide; retain the source hyperlinks for auditability.
